claims.text: The custom of saying 'bless you' after a sneeze likely originates from a combination of ancient Roman superstition (the soul could escape with a sneeze) and Pope Gregory I's response to the 6th-century plague (blessing sneezers as protective prayer for health).

evidence.quote:
someone sneezes why well it's
polite but how did this become a thing
some say this tradition dates back to
the ancient romans who thought that when
you sneezed your soul was ejected from
the body and a blessing was the only way
to protect it from being stolen by the
devil another theory is that pope
gregory who became pope after his
predecessor died from the plague would
say god bless you whenever someone
sneezed as a way of wishing them good
health another theory is that some
believed your heart would stop beating
for a brief moment during a sneeze and a
blessing encouraged the heart to start
beating again
you good
i didn't get it don't you hate that yeah
it's untight thank you
sup

claims.rationale: These are the two most commonly cited folk-etymologies for 'bless you'. Both have decent currency in folklore literature but neither has firm primary-source documentation. Pope Gregory I (590-604 AD) did preside during the bubonic plague and the bless-you-after-sneeze attribution is widely repeated, though direct contemporary evidence is thin. The 'soul escaping' Roman version is also folkloric tradition rather than verified Roman religious practice.

claims.text: Triskaidekaphobia (fear of 13) is so widespread in the West that 80% of high-rise buildings in the U.S. skip a 13th floor and Lufthansa and Air France skip row 13 — meanwhile, in much of East and Southeast Asia, tetraphobia (fear of 4) is comparably strong because the Chinese word for 'four' is nearly homophonic with the word for 'die'.

evidence.quote:
erstitions are all around us next
time you're in a tall building check out
the elevator eighty percent of high-rise
buildings in the u.s don't have a 13th
floor lufthansa and air france don't
have a 13th row fear of the number 13 is
called triscodecophobia but that's
mostly in the west in much of the east
and southeast asia you'll find
tetraphobia fear of the number four in
the chinese language and its subgroups
the word for and the word die are
pronounced almost the same
thank you so superstitions are universal
we've all had those days where from the
moment we wake up we're grumpy annoyed
at

claims.rationale: All sub-claims are well-supported. The Otis Elevator-cited estimate is roughly 85% of high-rises skip floor 13; '80%' is in range. Lufthansa and Air France do skip seat-row 13. The Chinese 四 (sì, four) / 死 (sǐ, die) near-homophony is the standard linguistic explanation for tetraphobia in CJK cultures, and floor-4 omission is common in Chinese, Korean, and Japanese buildings.

claims.text: The 'wrong side of the bed' idiom comes from ancient Roman superstition that the left side was unlucky and 'sinister' — the English word 'sinister' itself derives from Latin 'sinistra', meaning left.

evidence.quote:
the whole world and everything seems
against us we call this waking up on the
wrong side of the bed this is another
one that comes from ancient rome romans
like to wake up on the right side of the
bed
literally they believed that all things
on the left side were evil or sinister
in fact the word sinister comes from the
latin word sinistra so waking up on the
left side of the bed was literally a
sign of sinister things to come and if
you're having a bad day you can always
say tomorrow will be better then you say
knock on wood the superstition to knock
on wood appears to date back to the 19th
ce

claims.rationale: The etymology of 'sinister' from Latin sinister/sinistra (left, left-handed) is well established. Roman augury did treat the left side as inauspicious in some traditions (though Greek and Roman augury actually disagreed on which side meant what). The 'wrong side of the bed' idiom is widely traced to this Roman left-as-unlucky belief.

claims.text: The psychological mechanism behind superstition is anxiety control: a peer-reviewed study from the Association for Psychological Science showed that subjects who crossed their fingers for luck demonstrated measurable improvements in manual dexterity, memory function, and golf-putting performance — the ritual itself increased self-confidence enough to improve real-world results.

rt answer
is it's a coping mechanism for anxiety
the world is a crazy unpredictable place
it's only natural that we want to have
as much control of the unpredictable as
possible and for some people these
beliefs could be effective a study
reported by the association for
psychological science showed that people
who cross their fingers for luck had an
increase in manual dexterity and even an
improvement in memory function the study
concluded that crossing one's fingers
increased self-confidence enough so that
some people's golf scores improved hey
maybe crossing your fingers will help us
with the youtube algorithm
oh i know what we could try what throw a
coin in the fountain right throwing
coins and fountains also goes back to
ancient times when community wells were
built in villages coins w

claims.rationale: The host is referencing the well-known 2010 Damisch, Stoberock & Mussweiler study 'Keep Your Fingers Crossed!' published in Psychological Science (the journal of the Association for Psychological Science). It did find improvements in motor dexterity, anagram performance, and golf-putting through superstition-priming. However, the 'lucky charm' arm of that paper has had mixed replication results in subsequent meta-analyses; the underlying confidence/self-efficacy effect is more robust than the specific lucky-charm boost.

claims.text: Coin-throwing in fountains as a wish-making ritual descends from ancient Roman practice of throwing coins into community wells as offerings to gods — at one Northumberland, England well dedicated to the goddess Coventina, archaeologists have found over 16,000 Roman-era coins.

claims.rationale: Coventina's Well at Carrawburgh on Hadrian's Wall is a real archaeological site. The 1876 excavation by John Clayton recovered something on the order of 13,000-16,000 Roman coins along with altars and other votive offerings — '16,000' is at the high end of cited figures. The Coventina-as-water-goddess attribution and the well-as-votive-offering practice are both well-established. Connecting it directly to the modern fountain-coin custom is plausible cultural continuity though the chain is folkloric.

claims.text: Belief in life-extending magical waters is ancient: Alexander the Great searched for a river that cured old age (he died at just 32 from poison), and Ponce de León is legendarily said to have discovered Florida while searching for the Fountain of Youth — though most modern historians regard the latter as a 16th-century Spanish exaggeration.

believed in a river that cured old age
he was only 32 when he was poisoned to
death so who knows if he was right
legend says ponce de leon accidentally
discovered florida when he was searching
for the fountain of youth now most

these supernatural myths provide a sense

ponce leon they cooled the anxiety of

and put back together or that an

and teller's bullet catch in the teeth
is staged but wow is it a great tric

claims.rationale: Alexander the Great died in Babylon in 323 BC at age 32; whether he was poisoned vs. died of typhoid/malaria/alcohol is still debated by historians. The 'river of immortality' search is in the medieval Alexander Romance tradition, not solid contemporary history. The Ponce de León / Fountain of Youth myth is documented as later embellishment by Antonio de Herrera in 1535, post-dating Ponce's death — modern historians do generally treat it as legend rather than the actual motive for his Florida expedition.

claims.text: Harry Houdini campaigned publicly and before Congress against fraudulent spiritualist mediums, declaring séance-based communication with the dead 'a fraud from start to finish' — using his own stagecraft expertise to expose their tricks.

claims.rationale: Well-documented historical fact. Houdini did testify before the U.S. House Subcommittee on the Judiciary in 1926 in support of HR 8989, an anti-fortune-telling bill, and did spend much of his late career exposing spiritualist mediums. He published 'A Magician Among the Spirits' (1924) detailing his investigations. The 'fraud from start to finish' quote is widely attributed to him from this campaign.

claims.text: Superstitious behavior is endemic to professional baseball — Jason Giambi famously wore a gold thong to break batting slumps, and reliever Turk Wendell jumped over foul lines, brushed his teeth between innings, and chewed exactly four pieces of black licorice when pitching.

great pain and anxiety that's w

claims.rationale: Both anecdotes are real and well-circulated in baseball lore. Jason Giambi's gold-thong slump-busting ritual is documented in multiple sportswriting sources from the early 2000s. Turk Wendell's foul-line jump, tooth-brushing, and licorice-chewing routines are all real Wendell quirks much-noted during his Cubs/Mets/Phillies/Rockies career.

claims.text: Superstition and OCD are not the same: people with OCD are compelled to perform rituals to avoid distress (failure to perform causes pain and anxiety), while superstitions are voluntary anxiety-management rituals — and as long as superstitions don't become extreme, they are psychologically benign or even beneficial.

claims.rationale: Clinically accurate distinction. OCD compulsions are characterized by intrusive distress and the inability-to-not-perform; ordinary superstitions are autonomous, low-distress, and don't significantly impair functioning (DSM-5 criteria for OCD requires significant distress/impairment). The host's framing is consistent with mainstream clinical psychology.

claims.text: Performing small superstitious rituals (knocking on wood, throwing salt, crossing fingers) is psychologically healthy because it is, in effect, a focused mindfulness practice that quells anxiety and improves performance — the ritual works not by channeling supernatural force but by directing attention and self-confidence.

claims.rationale: The host's verdict integrates the empirical psychology results with a normative recommendation. The 'attention/confidence' framing is consistent with the cognitive psychology literature on ritual and self-efficacy. The 'healthy' framing is editorializing in line with that evidence — defensible but a personal recommendation rather than an objective truth claim.

dependencies.rationale: The Damisch finger-crossing study is the empirical backbone of this verdict.
Builds on the OCD-vs-superstition distinction to recommend benign practice.
